message.content:
You are verifier #2, independent. Read the ACTUAL code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay and decide if this is a TRUE production-readiness problem in current code (6 passes hardened). confirmed=true only if real, reproducible, worth fixing (not speculative/by-design).
J-002: Operator full display name ('[REDACTED]') is not scrubbed — leaks in 252 committed transcript files alongside redacted emails
File: scripts/capture_transcripts.py:38
VERIFIED. _SECRET_PATTERNS + _USERNAME_RE redact the operator's email and the bare home-dir username ('[REDACTED]'), and mask the username inside paths/slugs, but do NOT redact the operator's real full DISPLAY NAME ('[REDACTED]'), which is distinct operator PII appearing via git author strings, pyproject authors=[], and prose. Confirmed live and already committed: `grep -rc '[REDACTED]' transcripts/` returns 252 files with the name in cleartext, frequently directly beside a successfully-redacted email (e.g. `Author: [REDACTED] <[REDACTED]>`). `git log -1 --format=%an` returns '[REDACTED]', confirming this is the operator identity, not a coincidental token. De-identifying the email while leaving the adjacent full name defeats the de-identification purpose of the #F-024/#G-007 work. No test in tests/test_scripts.py covers full-name redaction (0 matches), confirming an unintentional gap rather than a documented scope decision.
PoC: 1) cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay  2) git log -1 --format='%an' -> '[REDACTED]' (operator PII)  3) grep -rc '[REDACTED]' transcripts/ -> 252 files with count >=1 (cleartext)  4) _scrub('Author: [REDACTED] <a@b.c>') -> 'Author: [REDACTED] <[REDACTED]>' (email masked, full name leaks).
Fix: Derive the operator display name (from `git config user.name` and/or a configurable ASSAY_SCRUB_NAMES env list) and add it to the redaction set, mirroring _username_pattern(): re.escape'd, word-boundaried, length-guarded, applied in _scrub() (skip if empty/too-short to avoid over-redacting common words). Add a test_scripts.py case asserting _scrub('Author: [REDACTED] <a@b.c>') masks BOTH name and email. Because the leak is already committed, re-run capture (or scrub history) to remediate existing transcripts/ files.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll verify this finding by examining the actual code and reproducing the claims.

content.content:
1	#!/usr/bin/env python3
2	"""Capture the active Claude session's JSONL transcript(s) into the repo for provenance.
3	
4	Run by the pre-commit hook (.githooks/pre-commit) so every commit pulls in an up-to-date
5	snapshot of the agent interactions/effort behind it — an auditable record alongside the work.
6	
7	What it captures: the *active* session (the most-recently-modified top-level transcript in the
8	Claude project dir) plus that session's ``<id>/`` subtree (subagents + workflow transcripts).
9	Other/unrelated sessions are left out. Credential-shaped secrets are redacted on the way in.
10	
11	Honest scope: transcripts still contain local filesystem paths and may contain the operator's
12	email and internal reasoning. If this repo is published, review/exclude ``transcripts/`` first
13	(see transcripts/README.md). The wheel never ships them (only ``src/assay_engine`` is packaged).
14	
15	Config: ASSAY_TRANSCRIPT_SRC overrides the source dir. Exits 0 on any error (never blocks a
16	commit).
17	"""
18	
19	from __future__ import annotations
20	
21	import datetime as _dt
22	import hashlib
23	import json
24	import os
25	import re
26	import sys
27	from pathlib import Path
28	
29	DEFAULT_SRC = Path.home() / ".claude" / "projects" / "-Users-[REDACTED]-hc-grc"
30	DEST = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent / "transcripts" / "sessions"
31	MANIFEST = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent / "transcripts" / "MANIFEST.json"
32	
33	# Credential-shaped secrets to redact (defense). Pass 3 (#F-024) extends this to operator PII —
34	# email addresses and the home-directory username embedded in filesystem paths — so committing
35	# transcripts as provenance (the campaign mandate) does not also publish the operator's identity.
36	# Reasoning content and the path STRUCTURE remain (legitimate provenance); see the README caveat
37	# for the full public-release consideration.
38	_SECRET_PATTERNS = [
39	    re.compile(r"sk-ant-[A-Za-z0-9_-]{2,}"),  # any sk-ant- token incl. oat01 OAuth + placeholders
40	    re.compile(r"\bgh[posru]_[A-Za-z0-9]{20,}\b"),  # GitHub tokens
41	    re.compile(r"\bAKIA[0-9A-Z]{16}\b"),  # AWS access key id
42	    # bearer tokens incl. base64url/base64 JWTs — must cover + / = or the token is split at the
43	    # first such char and only the leading fragment is redacted (pass 3, #F-051).
44	    re.compile(r"(?i)bearer\s+[A-Za-z0-9._\-+/=]{20,}"),
45	    # explicit OAuth/token env values: "...OAUTH_TOKEN":"<value>" / ...KEY=<value>
46	    re.compile(r'((?:OAUTH_TOKEN|_TOKEN|_KEY|_SECRET)"\s*:\s*")[^"]{8,}(")'),
47	    re.compile(r"((?:OAUTH_TOKEN|_TOKEN|_KEY|_SECRET)=)[^\s\"']{8,}"),
48	    # operator PII (#F-024): email addresses, and the username segment of a home path
49	    # (/Users/[REDACTED] /home/[REDACTED] — the path STRUCTURE is kept, only the identity is masked.
50	    re.compile(r"\b[A-Za-z0-9._%+-]+@[A-Za-z0-9.-]+\.[A-Za-z]{2,}\b"),  # email → [REDACTED]
51	    re.compile(r"(/(?:Users|home)/)[^/\s\"']+"),  # /Users/[REDACTED] → /Users/[REDACTED]    # The Claude project-dir SLUG (e.g. -Users-[REDACTED]-hc-grc): redact the username segment
53	    # regardless of length (#H-018) — the bare-token rule below skips short handles to avoid
54	    # over-redacting common words, but the slug form is unambiguous PII at any length.
55	    re.compile(r"(-(?:Users|home)-)[^-\s/\"']+"),  # -Users-[REDACTED]- → -Users-[REDACTED]-
56	]
57	
58	
59	def _username_pattern() -> re.Pattern[str] | None:
60	    """A redaction for the bare operator username wherever it appears (#G-007).
61	
62	    The /Users|/home path rule only masks the username inside a path; the same handle leaks via
63	    git author strings, prose, and the Claude project-dir slug (``-Users-[REDACTED]-...``). Derive the
64	    handle from the home directory and redact it as a standalone token too. Skipped for trivially
65	    short/None handles (e.g. a CI 'root') to avoid over-redacting common words.
66	    """
67	    try:
68	        name = Path.home().name
69	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 - never break capture over a missing home
70	        return None
71	    if not name or len(name) < 4:
72	        return None
73	    return re.compile(re.escape(name))
74	
75	
76	_USERNAME_RE = _username_pattern()
77	
78	
79	def _scrub(text: str) -> str:
80	    for pat in _SECRET_PATTERNS:
81	        if pat.groups == 2:
82	            text = pat.sub(r"\1[REDACTED]\2", text)
83	        elif pat.groups == 1:
84	            text = pat.sub(r"\1[REDACTED]", text)
85	        else:
86	            text = pat.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
87	    # Redact the bare operator username everywhere (#G-007), after the path rule so masked paths
88	    # don't re-expose it. Done last so it also catches it inside project-dir slugs / git authors.
89	    if _USERNAME_RE is not None:
90	        text = _USERNAME_RE.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
91	    return text
92	
93	
94	def _active_session(src: Path) -> Path | None:
95	    """The most-recently-modified top-level transcript = the live session."""
96	    candidates = sorted(src.glob("*.jsonl"), key=lambda p: p.stat().st_mtime, reverse=True)
97	    return candidates[0] if candidates else None
98	
99	
100	def _copy_scrubbed(srcf: Path, dstf: Path) -> dict | None:
101	    try:
102	        dstf.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
103	        raw = srcf.read_bytes()
104	        # Scrub EVERY text file, not only .jsonl (#CV-O-1): the session subtree also holds .json
105	        # workflow/subagent transcripts that carry the same operator PII + credential-shaped
106	        # secrets — the old suffix==".jsonl" gate copied those RAW, bypassing redaction entirely.
107	        # Decode strictly to classify: text → scrub; genuinely binary (rare here) → copy verbatim.
108	        try:
109	            text = raw.decode("utf-8")
110	        except UnicodeDecodeError:
111	            data = raw  # binary blob — nothing to scrub
112	        else:
113	            data = _scrub(text).encode("utf-8")
114	        if dstf.exists() and dstf.read_bytes() == data:
115	            pass  # unchanged — still report it in the manifest
116	        else:
117	            dstf.write_bytes(data)
118	        return {
119	            "path": str(dstf.relative_to(DEST.parent.parent)),
120	            "bytes": len(data),
121	            "sha256": hashlib.sha256(data).hexdigest(),
122	        }
123	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 — provenance capture must never break a commit
124	        return None
125	
126	
127	def main() -> int:
128	    src = Path(os.environ.get("ASSAY_TRANSCRIPT_SRC", str(DEFAULT_SRC)))
129	    if not src.is_dir():
130	        return 0
131	    session = _active_session(src)
132	    if session is None:
133	        return 0
134	    sid = session.stem
135	    captured: list[dict] = []
136	
137	    rec = _copy_scrubbed(session, DEST / f"{sid}.jsonl")
138	    if rec:
139	        captured.append(rec)
140	
141	    # the session's subtree: subagents/ + workflows/ transcripts
142	    subtree = src / sid
143	    if subtree.is_dir():
144	        for f in sorted(subtree.rglob("*")):
145	            if f.is_file():
146	                rel = f.relative_to(subtree)
147	                rec = _copy_scrubbed(f, DEST / sid / rel)
148	                if rec:
149	                    captured.append(rec)
150	
151	    try:
152	        MANIFEST.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
153	        MANIFEST.write_text(
154	            json.dumps(
155	                {
156	                    "captured_at": _dt.datetime.now(tz=_dt.timezone.utc).isoformat(),
157	                    "active_session": sid,
158	                    "n_files": len(captured),
159	                    "total_bytes": sum(c["bytes"] for c in captured),
160	                    "files": captured,
161	                },
162	                indent=2,
163	            )
164	            + "\n",
165	            encoding="utf-8",
166	        )
167	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001
168	        return 0
169	    print(f"captured {len(captured)} transcript file(s) for session {sid}")
170	    return 0
171	
172	
173	if __name__ == "__main__":
174	    sys.exit(main())
175

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && git log -1 --format='%an' && echo "---" && grep -rc '[REDACTED]' transcripts/ 2>/dev/null | grep -v ':0$' | wc -l

content.text: Both claims confirmed. Let me verify the email-redacted-but-name-present pattern and check tests.

    test(hardening): close remaining gaps from the hardening review
    
    Independent review of the real-backend hardening confirmed it genuine but found
    8 remaining gaps concentrated where failure is expensive and CI gives false
    security. Closed the high-value ones:
    
    - T3 error classification (was the most dangerous untested code — rate-limit /
      auth / error / empty mapping, runnable before only via a metered call): now
      covered in CI by driving the REAL _drain logic with a synthetic claude_agent_sdk
      (scripted AssistantMessage/RateLimitEvent/ResultMessage stream) — no metered
      call, no service. Covers the 429-not-clobbered, allowed-vs-rejected, auth, and
      empty-reply branches. seam.py coverage 66% -> 91%.
    - VectorStore was declared but unimplemented (vapor): added QdrantVectorStore
      (deterministic id->UUID mapping, original id in payload, cosine) implementing
      the Protocol, with a live upsert+nearest-neighbour-query test against running
      qdrant. vectorstore 81% -> 97%.
    - Bulk-tier error mapping now tested against a REAL ollama "model not found"
      (-> PermanentReasoningError), not just mocks.
    - Real-threads concurrency test for the bounded reasoning pool: saturate every
      worker slot, assert fail-fast + slot release under genuine contention.
    - Observability flush-on-exit/SIGTERM wiring tested (atexit + SIGTERM call
      force_flush/shutdown) — lost-spans-on-container-stop risk.
    - confirm_whole_corpus guard branches (stability_threshold range, empty/
      non-finite resamples) covered.
    - CI integration lane now runs postgres + qdrant SERVICE CONTAINERS so the live
      checkpointer + vector-store tests execute in CI (not just locally); ollama and
      the metered frontier tier honestly skip in CI.
    
    Coverage 95% overall; 265 passed, 5 skipped; ruff + mypy --strict clean (34 files).

===MSG===
test(hardening): close remaining gaps from the hardening review

Independent review of the real-backend hardening confirmed it genuine but found
8 remaining gaps concentrated where failure is expensive and CI gives false
security. Closed the high-value ones:

- T3 error classification (was the most dangerous untested code — rate-limit /
  auth / error / empty mapping, runnable before only via a metered call): now
  covered in CI by driving the REAL _drain logic with a synthetic claude_agent_sdk
  (scripted AssistantMessage/RateLimitEvent/ResultMessage stream) — no metered
  call, no service. Covers the 429-not-clobbered, allowed-vs-rejected, auth, and
  empty-reply branches. seam.py coverage 66% -> 91%.
- VectorStore was declared but unimplemented (vapor): added QdrantVectorStore
  (deterministic id->UUID mapping, original id in payload, cosine) implementing
  the Protocol, with a live upsert+nearest-neighbour-query test against running
  qdrant. vectorstore 81% -> 97%.
- Bulk-tier error mapping now tested against a REAL ollama "model not found"
  (-> PermanentReasoningError), not just mocks.
- Real-threads concurrency test for the bounded reasoning pool: saturate every
  worker slot, assert fail-fast + slot release under genuine contention.
- Observability flush-on-exit/SIGTERM wiring tested (atexit + SIGTERM call
  force_flush/shutdown) — lost-spans-on-container-stop risk.
- confirm_whole_corpus guard branches (stability_threshold range, empty/
  non-finite resamples) covered.
- CI integration lane now runs postgres + qdrant SERVICE CONTAINERS so the live
  checkpointer + vector-store tests execute in CI (not just locally); ollama and
  the metered frontier tier honestly skip in CI.

Coverage 95% overall; 265 passed, 5 skipped; ruff + mypy --strict clean (34 files).

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) <[REDACTED]>

===PREV===
test(hardening): exercise the platform against REAL backends + fix what mocks hid

Proper hardening (prior "hardening" was adversarial code-review + mocked tests —
the real backend code paths had NEVER executed). Installed every extra and the
local services and actually ran the platform:

- New integration suite (tests/integration/, skips cleanly when an extra/service
  is absent so the dependency-free lane stays green):
  - reasoning: REAL Ollama bulk call + run_json; high-stakes (T3) availability;
    a guarded real frontier call. (Manually validated T3 end-to-end returns text —
    the critical content-block fix had never run against the real Agent SDK.)
  - orchestration: a REAL langgraph graph parks at a gate interrupt, resumes to
    completion, RE-PROMPTS on a bad resume (validates the #G1 "never bricked" fix
    under genuine interrupt/checkpoint-replay semantics), and records rejections.
  - observability: real MLflow lifecycle (sqlite), real OTel baggage attach/detach,
    real Phoenix provider bootstrap + idempotency, OtelTracer span emission.
  - persistence: real MemorySaver graph-state round-trip; Qdrant + Postgres when up.
  - methodology end-to-end: corpus -> primitives -> reproducible BaselineArtifact
    -> locked hypothesis -> confirm -> verdict, composed for real.

- Real deficiencies the mocks hid, now fixed:
  - cosine_similarity_matrix was pure-Python O(n²·d) — unusable at corpus scale.
    Added a numpy-accelerated path (optional `baseline` extra) with a pure
    fallback and a pure-vs-numpy agreement test.
  - mypy against the REAL psycopg/langgraph types caught a dict/tuple row-type
    mismatch in the Postgres checkpointer (masked by ignore_missing_imports);
    fixed with a documented cast (row_factory=dict_row makes it true at runtime).
  - two brittle "fails-loud-without-extra" tests broke once the extra was present;
    guarded to skip when installed (real-present behavior covered by integration).

- CI: split into a dependency-free `core` lane (no extras: ruff+mypy+pytest on
  3.11/3.12/3.13, integration skips) and an `integration` lane (all extras:
  mypy against real types + integration tests + coverage). Verified the dep-free
  lane in a clean venv: 239 passed, 13 skipped.

Full lane (extras + local services): 248 passed, 6 skipped; ruff + mypy --strict
clean (34 files). Coverage of reasoning seam rose from 66% as the real backend
paths now execute.

    feat(engine): scaffold dataset-agnostic engine skeleton + methodology core
    
    Stand up the assay_engine package per ARCHITECTURE.md/ADR-0002. The engine
    imports no dataset specifics; adapters implement the contracts it calls.
    
    Contracts (engine <-> adapter boundary):
    - canonical schema (Corpus/Unit/Relation), ingestion-parser Protocol,
      optional external-claims source (quarantined per Firewall A),
      feature-builder Protocol, study definition + two modes.
    
    Methodology core (concrete, enforced in code):
    - three verdicts (supported/contradicted/indeterminate);
    - typed, falsifiable hypotheses with lock/timestamp state;
    - the two firewalls as runtime primitives (ClaimBlindGuard,
      DiscoverConfirmSplit) that raise FirewallViolation, not docstrings;
    - confirmatory machinery (locking gate, held-out + null/permutation+

commit 91dfb1a4a4393317852caac2a5345cfc115348eb
Author: [REDACTED] <[REDACTED]>
Date:   Tue Jun 16 23:52:20 2026 -0400

    fix(hardening-p4): batch B — concurrency, reasoning-tier & serialization (G-002,003,004)
    
    Pass-4 confirmed highs (2-agent verified, revert-discriminated):
    
    - G-002 (high) RESET lock_timeout on the bootstrap connection before it returns to the
      pool. The migration-lock bootstrap set lock_timeout at SESSION scope (autocommit, so
      is_local couldn't help); that connection was then reused for all later checkpoint ops,
      silently inheriting a non-default lock_timeout the operator never configured.
    - G-003 (high) BULK tier inner bound made robust + total. BULK relies on the HTTP client
      timeout (the sync-tier counterpart to HIGH_STAKES's anyio.fail_after) since a pool worker
      blocked in invoke() can't be interrupted by the outer _with_timeout. A bare float set every
      httpx phase to BULK_TIMEOUT, so connect+read could total ~2x and exceed the outer
      BULK_TIMEOUT+10 bound, leaking the slot. Now an explicit httpx.Timeout(read=BULK_TIMEOUT,
      connect<=10) keeps the total within the outer headroom.
    - G-004 (high) unfreeze() frozenset fallback for unorderable mixed types now sorts by a
      STABLE (type name, repr) key instead of raw set-iteration order (hash-seed dependent →
      non-reproducible serialized output across processes). Verified: the reverted version fails
      under PYTHONHASHSEED=7.
    
    Gates: ruff + ruff format --check + mypy --strict clean; 508 passed, 5 skipped.
    
    Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) <[REDACTED]>

diff --git a/src/assay_engine/_frozen.py b/src/assay_engine/_frozen.py
index ee92814..9f3ab7c 100644
--- a/src/assay_engine/_frozen.py
+++ b/src/assay_engine/_frozen.py
@@ -151,11 +151,14 @@ def unfreeze(value: Any) -> Any:
     if isinstance(value, (FrozenDict, Mapping)):
         return {k: unfreeze(v) for k, v in value.items()}
     if isinstance(value, frozenset):
-        # sort when elements are mutually orderable; otherwise keep insertion-free stable repr
+        # sort for reproducible output; when elements are not mutually orderable (mixed types),
+        # fall back to a STABLE key — (type name, repr) — rather than raw set iteration order, which
+        # is hash-seed/insertion dependent and would make serialized output non-reproducible across
+        # processes (pass 4, #G-004).
         try:
             return [unfreeze(v) for v in sorted(value)]
         except TypeError:
-            return [unfreeze(v) for v in value]
+            return [unfreeze(v) for v in sorted(value, key=lambda x: (type(x).__name__, repr(x)))]
     if isinstance(value, (list, tuple)):
         return [unfreeze(v) for v in value]
     return value

commit 82f58cadb91e353ba497067fdba21a90080e68e5
Author: [REDACTED] <[REDACTED]>
Date:   Tue Jun 16 22:25:38 2026 -0400

    fix(hardening-p3): batch 5 — performance, with two scoped declines (5 findings)
    
    Pass-3 confirmed findings (2-agent verified, revert-discriminated):
    
    - F-034 freeze_mapping() is idempotent: an already-FrozenDict is returned unchanged so
      its lazily-cached hash survives (re-wrapping forced an O(N*depth) re-hash). Affects
      every ProvenanceEntry/DeterminismRecord that re-freezes a payload holding FrozenDicts.
    - F-016 corpus_fingerprint streams JSON into SHA-256 via JSONEncoder.iterencode instead
      of materializing one giant string — lower peak RAM at hash time. Proven BYTE-IDENTICAL
      (a pinned-hash test guards the exact fingerprint so the change — or any future one —
      that alters it fails loudly).
    - F-017 the O(R*N) resample-stability loop has a numpy fast-path (single vectorized R×N
      comparison) with a pure-Python fallback. Verified to return EXACTLY the pure-Python
      value across 300 randomized cases — a speed optimization, never a semantic change.
    - F-036 run_study(verify_trail=False) lets a perf-sensitive caller skip the O(N)
      end-of-run re-verification of an in-memory trail it just built; default stays verify-on.
    
    Scoped DECLINES (documented in code, the finding's remedy can't preserve a contract):
    - F-035 (FeatureMatrix numpy validation): np.asarray UPCASTS Python bool→numeric
      ([True,1.0]→float64), so no numpy form preserves the bool-exclusion contract (#149)
      AND numpy-float-scalar acceptance. Kept the authoritative pure-Python check, micro-
      optimized the per-cell locals. (Blueprint has no real corpus yet — 100k×100 is not a
      current workload.)
    - F-016 double-computation (engine + builder each hash the corpus): not closed — would
      require an invasive BaselineBuilder protocol change across all adapters; the two hashes
      are verified equal at the pipeline boundary, so it is correctness-safe as is.
    
    Gates: ruff + ruff format --check + mypy --strict clean; 498 passed, 5 skipped.
    F-016 + F-034 revert-discrimination verified; F-017 equivalence proven numerically.
    
    Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) <[REDACTED]>

diff --git a/src/assay_engine/_frozen.py b/src/assay_engine/_frozen.py
index 2152226..ee92814 100644
--- a/src/assay_engine/_frozen.py
+++ b/src/assay_engine/_frozen.py
@@ -127,7 +127,15 @@ def freeze(value: Any) -> Any:
 
 
 def freeze_mapping(value: Mapping[str, Any]) -> FrozenDict:
-    """Normalize a mapping field to a deep-immutable :class:`FrozenDict`."""
+    """Normalize a mapping field to a deep-immutable :class:`FrozenDict`.
+
+    Idempotent (pass 3, #F-034): an already-:class:`FrozenDict` value is returned unchanged so
+    its lazily-cached hash survives. Re-wrapping (e.g. when ``ProvenanceEntry.__post_init__``
+    freezes a payload that already holds FrozenDict values) would otherwise allocate a fresh
+    FrozenDict with ``_hash=None`` and force an O(N·depth) re-hash on next use.
+    """
+    if isinstance(value, FrozenDict):
+        return value
     return FrozenDict({k: freeze(v) for k, v in value.items()})

content.text: All claims confirmed. The PoC is fully reproducible. Let me verify the `_scrub` behavior directly.

input.command:
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && python3 -c "
import sys; sys.path.insert(0, 'scripts')
import capture_transcripts as ct
print(repr(ct._scrub('Author: [REDACTED] <a@b.c>')))
print('USERNAME_RE redacts:', repr(ct._scrub('[REDACTED]')))
"

content.text: Interesting — even the email is NOT redacted here. That's because `a@b.c` has a single-char TLD (`.c`), which doesn't match `[A-Za-z]{2,}`. Let me test with a realistic email to confirm the core claim (name leaks while a real email is masked).

input.command:
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && python3 -c "
import sys; sys.path.insert(0, 'scripts')
import capture_transcripts as ct
print(repr(ct._scrub('Author: [REDACTED] <[REDACTED]>')))
"

content.text:
Confirmed. With a real email the email is masked but "[REDACTED]" leaks in cleartext — exactly the described defeat of the de-identification intent. The transcripts already show this committed (`Author: [REDACTED] <[REDACTED]>` in 252 files).

Verification summary:
- `git log -1 --format='%an'` → `[REDACTED]` (operator identity, not coincidental). Confirmed.
- `grep -rc '[REDACTED]' transcripts/` → 252 files with cleartext name. Confirmed.
- Live cleartext beside redacted email in committed transcripts (`Author: [REDACTED] <[REDACTED]>`, plus `Co-authored-by: [REDACTED] <[REDACTED]>`). Confirmed.
- `_scrub('Author: [REDACTED] <[REDACTED]>')` masks email, leaks full name. Confirmed.
- `pyproject.toml` authors uses `thehipsterciso` (not the real name), so that specific vector isn't present, but git author/co-author strings and prose carry the full name throughout transcripts.
- No test coverage for full-name redaction (`grep` of test_scripts.py → 0). Confirmed gap, not documented by-design.

The redaction set deliberately targets operator PII (email + username via #F-024/#G-007). The full display name is equivalent-tier PII, distinct from the username, and is verifiably leaking. This is a real, reproducible de-identification gap. The PoC's email example used `<a@b.c>` which wouldn't redact the email either due to TLD length, but with a realistic email the described behavior (name leaks, email masked) holds exactly.

Severity: This is operator PII leakage already committed to a repo intended (per the module docstring/README) to be reviewable for public release. It's reliability/privacy, not exploitable code execution. Aligns with how the email/username leak was treated — medium.

input.reasoning:
Reproduced all core claims in current code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay. (1) `git log -1 --format='%an'` returns '[REDACTED]' — the operator identity. (2) `grep -rc '[REDACTED]' transcripts/` returns 252 files with cleartext occurrences. (3) Committed transcripts contain the full name directly beside a successfully-redacted email, e.g. `Author: [REDACTED] <[REDACTED]>` and `Co-authored-by: [REDACTED] <[REDACTED]>`. (4) Running the real `_scrub()` on `Author: [REDACTED] <[REDACTED]>` yields `Author: [REDACTED] <[REDACTED]>` — email masked, full display name leaked in cleartext.

scripts/capture_transcripts.py:38-91 deliberately redacts operator PII: email (line 50), home-dir username in paths (51), the project-dir slug username (55), and the bare username token via _username_pattern() (59-90). The operator's full DISPLAY name is distinct, equivalent-tier PII and is NOT in the redaction set, so it leaks via git author/co-author strings and prose. tests/test_scripts.py has zero coverage for full-name redaction, confirming an unintentional gap rather than a documented by-design scope decision — the module docstring and #F-024/#G-007 work establish that de-identifying operator identity (so committed provenance does not publish it) IS the intent, which leaving the adjacent full name defeats.

One inaccuracy in the PoC: its literal example `_scrub('Author: [REDACTED] <a@b.c>')` does NOT mask the email either, because `.c` is a 1-char TLD that fails the `[A-Za-z]{2,}` email pattern; with a realistic email the described behavior (email masked, name leaked) holds exactly. Also pyproject.toml authors uses 'thehipsterciso', not the real name, so that specific listed vector is absent — but the git-author and prose vectors are real and pervasive (252 files). These do not undermine the core finding.

Severity medium, consistent with how the prior email/username leaks (#F-024/#G-007) were treated: this is operator-PII leakage already committed to a repo whose own docstring/README frame it for public-release review, not an exploitable code-execution flaw. The fix is straightforward (derive name from `git config user.name` / a configurable ASSAY_SCRUB_NAMES list, re.escape + word-boundary + length-guard, mirror _username_pattern, add a test), and remediating committed files requires re-capture or history scrub.

content.text: Verification complete. Finding J-002 is CONFIRMED as a true production-readiness issue (medium severity). Details returned via StructuredOutput.
